figures.caption: Fig. 1. Yoli: A physical board game for young children. Different tiles are selected sequentially to achieve a goal. The board is connected to an app that is used by the teacher who supervises the game session. The app provides AI capabilities to the board.
Fig. 8. Average story quality score after each Writer–Editor iteration. Scores increase consistently across iterations for all models used as Editors.
Fig. 9. Each line indicates how likely it is for each critic to reach its highest score as a function of the number of Writer-Editor loops.

Fig. 2. Writer’s prompt for the first loop. NOTE: For all prompts in this paper, some keywords have been marked in bold to improve readability. All input parameters (e.g., protagonist) for the prompts are enclosed between curly brackets, and these parameters are replaced by the corresponding tiles at execution time.
Fig. 4. Editor’s prompt for the first loop. Part 2/2.
Fig. 3. Editor’s prompt for the first loop. Part 1/2.
Fig. 5. Writer’s prompt for the subsequent loops.

Fig. 6. Editor’s prompt for the subsequent loops. Notice that this prompt starts with Editor’s prompt from the first loop. This placeholder will be replaced by the actual Editor’s prompt used in the first loop. Recall that the Editor’s prompt template from the first loop is shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. 4.
Fig. 7. Illustration of the Writer-Editor Loops used in the simulation study.
